tldr: Camunda is a BPMN process orchestration platform; Apidog is an all-in-one API design, testing and documentation tool. They overlap only at the edges — moving means rethinking workflow logic as APIs plus an external orchestrator.
why_switch: Shift focus from long-running workflows to API-first product development
Reduce operational overhead of running a workflow engine
Standardize the team on a single API design / testing surface
Lower licensing and infrastructure spend on Camunda Platform
Adopt OpenAPI as the system of record instead of BPMN models
what_to_export: Process variable schemas and message contracts
export_method: Camunda treats BPMN/DMN diagrams as portable XML, so begin by pulling those files directly from source control or Camunda Modeler. Generic steps: 1) collect every BPMN, DMN and form file from each project; 2) document process variables and message names so they can be expressed as API contracts; 3) export historical process instance data via the operations / history APIs if you need audit retention; 4) inventory external task workers and the services they call; 5) record identity mappings and connector credentials. Camunda's open-standard BPMN keeps portability high, but anything tied to Camunda-specific extensions, connectors or Tasklist UI will not move cleanly. Confirm the current Camunda export tooling and history API in the official docs before relying on it.
import_method: Apidog does not execute workflows — it manages API specs, mock servers, tests and documentation. Plan accordingly: 1) translate each BPMN process boundary into one or more API contracts and author them as OpenAPI specs in Apidog; 2) create projects/folders that mirror your service boundaries; 3) import existing OpenAPI files via Apidog's import flow; 4) recreate test scenarios and example data that previously lived as process instances; 5) wire CI to Apidog's CLI for automated test runs; 6) if you still need orchestration, pick a separate engine (Temporal, Airflow, a lightweight scheduler) — Apidog will not replace that capability. Validate authentication, environment variables and team permissions before cutover. Verify current import formats and CLI capabilities in the latest Apidog documentation.

concept_mapping.target: Frontend app or separate forms tool
Documented business rule, evaluated outside Apidog

gotchas: Apidog cannot execute long-running, stateful workflows — pair it with a dedicated orchestrator if those use cases remain.
Historical process instance data has compliance value; export and archive it before tearing Camunda down.
BPMN-native concepts (timers, compensation, multi-instance) do not map to a request/response API model and must be redesigned.
Camunda connectors and Tasklist UI have no direct equivalent and will require replacement components.
Identity providers, RBAC and audit logging differ — re-test access controls end-to-end after migration.
Performance characteristics of an API-driven design vary from a workflow engine; load-test critical paths early.

email.subject: Migration plan: Camunda to Apidog over the next 14 days
email.body:
Hi team,

We're starting a 14-day migration off Camunda. Apidog is our new home for API design, testing and docs, but it does not execute workflows — anything stateful or long-running will move to a dedicated orchestrator (decision pending). Plan:

1) Days 1-3: inventory every BPMN/DMN model, form, worker and connector. Export models from source control and snapshot historical process data for audit.
2) Days 4-8: translate process boundaries into OpenAPI specs in Apidog, recreate test scenarios, and stand up CI against Apidog's CLI. Stub orchestration-only behaviour for now.
3) Days 9-12: identify the residual workflows that still need an engine and document the chosen orchestrator decision so we don't lose those use cases.
4) Days 13-14: dual-run critical flows, then freeze writes to Camunda and switch traffic.

Please flag any process that relies on Camunda-specific features (timers, compensation, Tasklist UI) so we can plan replacements rather than discover gaps in production. Reply with concerns by end of week.

Thanks,
Your customer
